transcript.presenting_concern: Distress tolerance skills for managing hypomanic impulsivity

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Christina, good to see you. Diary card first — let me take a look.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Here. Fair warning, Thursday and Friday were not my best days.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I see. Mood went up to an 8 on Thursday — that's in the hypomanic range. And then impulse spending was marked on both Thursday and Friday. Tell me what happened.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So I'd been feeling really good for a few days — like, productive, energized, sleeping less but not feeling tired. By Wednesday I recognized the pattern — "oh shit, this might be hypomania starting." But it felt SO GOOD after being depressed for three weeks that I kind of... let it ride.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You recognized it but chose not to intervene. What was the thought process?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The thought was "maybe this is just what normal feels like and I've been depressed so long I forgot." The justification my brain made was "this isn't hypomania, this is just me finally feeling like myself." Classic.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And what happened Thursday and Friday?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thursday I spent eight hundred dollars online. New clothes, stuff for the apartment, a subscription I don't need. Friday I spent another four hundred. I also texted my ex at 2 AM Thursday — like six texts in a row, very intense, telling him I missed him and we should try again.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Did he respond?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Thank god. I woke up Friday morning, saw the texts, and wanted to die of embarrassment. That's when the mood started crashing back down. Saturday I returned what I could — got about half the money back. Deleted the texts to my ex. Felt horrible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So let's map the chain here. Mood elevation began gradually — you noticed it by Wednesday. Rather than implementing coping at that point, you let the elevated mood continue because it felt good. By Thursday, impulsivity was escalating — spending, impulsive contact with your ex. Friday morning the consequences became apparent. Saturday you did damage control. Is that the sequence?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. And the thing is, I KNOW this pattern. This is like the fifth time. I even have the stupid acronym — STOP — and I didn't use it. When I'm in that elevated state, using skills feels like it'll ruin the good feeling.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's one of the unique challenges of bipolar with DBT. In most emotional dysregulation, the emotion you're trying to manage feels BAD — you're motivated to reduce it. But hypomania feels GOOD, so using distress tolerance to bring yourself down feels like self-punishment. It's like asking yourself to get off a rollercoaster that's fun in the moment but you know crashes at the end.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Exactly. In the moment I'm like "why would I stop feeling amazing?" But the crash always comes. Always. And it always takes something with it — money, relationships, my self-respect.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So let's be strategic. What if we reframe the intervention point? Instead of "stop feeling good," what if the skill is "protect my future self from consequences I know are coming"?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's different. That's not about the feeling, it's about the behavior.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. You can FEEL the energy and positivity — we're not trying to eliminate that. We're trying to prevent the impulsive BEHAVIORS that come with it. Feeling energized and spending $1,200 in two days are two different things. One is a mood state; the other is a choice, even if it doesn't feel like one in the moment.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. So what's the concrete skill here? Because "just don't spend money" doesn't work when my brain is telling me I deserve everything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's build a hypomanic action plan — a set of pre-committed rules that go into effect when you identify mood elevation. Rule one: What if you gave your credit card to someone you trust — your sister maybe — with instructions to hold it when you text her a code word meaning "I'm going up"?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She'd do that. She's seen the fallout. The code word could be "rollercoaster."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Rule two: When elevated, no texting exes or making major social decisions. You put a 48-hour delay on any significant outreach. The feeling of "I need to text him RIGHT NOW" — by definition, if you wait 48 hours and still want to, it's less likely to be impulsive.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The 48-hour rule. Okay. I can put a note on my phone lock screen or something.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Rule three: Sleep protection. When you notice you're sleeping less but not tired, that's your earliest warning sign. That's when you implement sleep hygiene aggressively — melatonin, screen off by 10, dark room. Sometimes protecting sleep can prevent a full hypomanic episode from developing.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The sleep thing is real. Looking back, I was at six hours Monday, five hours Tuesday, four hours Wednesday... and by Thursday I was invincible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the action plan activates at the SLEEP signal, not at the spending signal. By the time you're spending impulsively, you're already deep in it. The intervention needs to happen days earlier.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Sleep drops below six hours → text my sister the code word, give her my card, set my phone alarm for bedtime, implement 48-hour rule on any big decisions or relationship contact. Is that the plan?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the plan. Let's write it on a card. Now — what about the aftermath? You said you felt horrible Saturday. Where are you now emotionally?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Mildly depressed. Maybe a 4 on the mood scale. The shame is heavy. I feel like I'm broken and I'll never be stable.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's check that "never be stable" thought. In the last three months, how many hypomanic episodes have you had?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: This one and one in early April. So two.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And between them — those weeks in the middle — were you impulsively spending, texting exes, sleeping four hours?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. I was just... living. Working, seeing friends, normal stuff.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the statement "I'll never be stable" is not supported by the evidence. You HAVE stability — the majority of your time is spent in a functional range. You have episodes that disrupt that stability, and we're building skills to catch those episodes earlier and reduce their damage. You are not your episodes.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [exhale] Okay. Both/and. I'm a person who has bipolar episodes AND I'm mostly stable AND I'm learning to manage them better. All three true.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: All three true. Now, the returns you made Saturday — that's wise mind action. The embarrassment about the texts — did anything actually bad happen from them?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. He didn't respond. Probably thinks I'm crazy but honestly, that ship sailed years ago.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Can you practice radical acceptance on that? The texts were sent. You can't un-send them. They didn't cause harm. The discomfort is the consequence, and it will fade.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "It is what it is." I hate that phrase but it's radical acceptance basically.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. For homework: write the action plan card and keep it in your wallet. Text your sister and set up the code word system. Track your sleep hours on the diary card as an early warning metric. And practice self-compassion for the episode — not condoning the behavior, but acknowledging you're human and learning.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I can do all that. Thanks, Dr. Morrison. I always feel less catastrophic after these sessions.

goals: Distress tolerance skills for managing hypomanic impulsivity
